title: Theo of Golden
author: Allen Levi
blurb: Theo of Golden is a beautifully crafted story about the power of creative generosity, the importance of wonder to a purposeful life, and the far-reaching possibilities of anonymous kindness.

title: Project Hail Mary
author: Andy Weir
blurb: An irresistible interstellar adventure as only Andy Weir could deliver, Project Hail Mary is a tale of discovery, speculation, and survival to rival The Martian—while taking us to places it never dreamed of going.

title: The Correspondent
author: Virginia Evans
blurb: Discover the word-of-mouth hit hailed by Ann Patchett as “A cause for celebration”—an intimate novel about the transformative power of the written word and the beauty of slowing down to reconnect with the people we love.

title: Yesteryear: A GMA Book Club Pick: A Novel
author: Caro Claire Burke
blurb: A traditional American woman, a “tradwife” influencer, suddenly awakens in the brutal reality of 1855—where she must unravel whether this living nightmare is an elaborate hoax, a twisted reality show, or something far more sinister in this sensational debut novel.

title: Purple State
author: Dana Perino
blurb: The #1 bestselling author, popular Fox News host, and former White House press secretary, Dana Perino, reaches new heights with her first novel—a smart, politically and romantically charged story where red meets blue, and love is never black and white.

title: King of Gluttony
author: Ana Huang
blurb: The sixth book in the deliciously steamy Kings of Sin series from #1 New York Times bestselling author Ana Huang, where bad boy billionaires must atone for their sins in order to earn their love stories.

title: The Dinner Party: A Pick Your Poison Adventure
author: Freida McFadden
blurb: Your friend has offered you a job working as a waitress at a dinner party... at an isolated mansion way out on Peyton's Peak. The money will be enough to cover two months' rent and get you back on your feet. It's a golden opportunity that will solve all your problems. But the whole thing sounds scetchy. The money is great, but there's got to be some soert of awful catch. You should probably turn it down, right??? As it turns out, the choice is yours.

title: Verity
author: Colleen Hoover
blurb: Whose truth is the lie? Stay up all night reading the sensational psychological thriller that has readers obsessed—the inspiration for the major motion picture starring Anne Hathaway and Dakota Johnson.

title: The Last Letter
author: Rebecca Yarros
blurb: If you’re reading this, well, you know the last-letter drill. You made it. I didn’t. Get off the guilt train, because I know if there was any chance you could have saved me, you would have.

title: Remarkably Bright Creatures: A Novel
author: Shelby Van Pelt
blurb: A charming, witty and compulsively readable exploration of friendship, reckoning, and hope that traces a widow's unlikely connection with a giant Pacific octopus.

title: Sunrise on the Reaping (A Hunger Games Novel)
author: Suzanne Collins
blurb: The phenomenal fifth book in the Hunger Games series! When you’ve been set up to lose everything you love, what is there left to fight for?

title: The Outsiders
author: S. E. Hinton
blurb: The Outsiders is a dramatic and enduring work of fiction that laid the groundwork for the YA genre. S. E. Hinton’s classic story of a boy who finds himself on the outskirts of regular society remains as powerful today as it was the day it was first published.

title: The Giver
author: Lois Lowry
blurb: Life in the community where Jonas lives is idyllic. Designated birthmothers produce newchildren, who are assigned to appropriate family units. Citizens are assigned their partners and their jobs. No one thinks to ask questions. Everyone obeys. Everyone is the same. Except Jonas.

title: Lord of the Flies
author: William Golding
blurb: At the dawn of the next world war, a plane crashes on an uncharted island, stranding a group of schoolboys. At first, with no adult supervision, their freedom is something to celebrate. This far from civilization they can do anything they want. Anything. But as order collapses, as strange howls echo in the night, as terror begins its reign, the hope of adventure seems as far removed from reality as the hope of being rescued.

title: Hatchet
author: Gary Paulsen
publisher: Simon & Schuster Books for Young Readers
blurb: Thirteen-year-old Brian Robeson, haunted by his secret knowledge of his mother’s infidelity, is traveling by single-engine plane to visit his father for the first time since the divorce. When the plane crashes, killing the pilot, the sole survivor is Brian. He is alone in the Canadian wilderness with nothing but his clothing, a tattered windbreaker, and the hatchet his mother had given him as a present.

title: Oh, the Places You'll Go!
author: Dr. Seuss
publisher: Random House Books for Young Readers
blurb: The inspiring and timeless message encourages readers to find the success that lies within, no matter what challenges they face. A perennial favorite and a perfect gift for anyone starting a new phase in their life!

title: I Love you to the Moon and Back
author: Amelia Hepworth and Tim Warnes
blurb: Show your child just how strong your love is every minute of the day with this sweet, gentle rhyme that's perfect for sharing. Now in a jacketed hardcover format!

title: The Very Hungry Caterpillar
author: Eric Carle
blurb: Follow along as one of nature’s loveliest marvels eats his way through an amazing variety of foods as he prepares to grow into a beautiful butterfly! Children will love the strikingly bold, colorful pictures against a simple text in large, clear type.

title: The Rainbow Fish
author: Marcsus Pfister
blurb: Eye-catching foil stamping, glittering on every page, will delight every child, but it is the universal message at the heart of this simple story about a beautiful fish who learns to make friends by sharing his most prized possessions that gives this book its enduring value.

title: Brown Bear, Brown Bear, What Do You See?
author: Bill Martin Jr. and Eric Carle
blurb: A beloved children’s story, now available in beginning reader format. With the important pre-reading concepts of rhyme, rhythm, and repetition, this book has long been used as beginning readers. Now, with a 6” x 9” trim and a classic beginning reader layout, children will have a more “grown up” version to read by themselves.

title: Invincible: Defy Your Genetic Destiny to Live Better, Longer
author: Florence Comite, MD
blurb: Discover a proven path to optimal health for the rest of your long life with this groundbreaking book revealing how you can harness the power of precision medicine to live healthier longer.

title: The Jesus Discoveries: 10 Historic Finds That Bring Us Face-to-Face with Jesus
author: Jeremiah J. Johnston
blurb: In this eye-opening, faith-fortifying book from acclaimed New Testament scholar and minister Jeremiah Johnston, you'll encounter ten of the most compelling historic finds that corroborate the truth claims found in the Bible regarding who Jesus said he was, when and where He lived, His claims and aims, how His life ended, and how that wasn't the end of His story.

title: St. Louis de Montfort's Total Consecration to Jesus through Mary
author: St. Louis de Montfort
blurb: This new translation strives to be ever-faithful to St. Louis de Montfort's classic work, while also avoiding unfamiliar phrases and terms. Thee's and thou's are a beautiful version of the English language, but might sometimes feel foreign. Scott Smith hopes that this beautiful new translation will bring St. Louis de Montfort's life-changing Marian consecration to even more people.

title: Murdle: Volume 1
author: G. T. Karber
blurb: G. T. Karber, the creator of the popular online daily mystery game Murdle, presents the first collection of 100 original murder mystery logic puzzles―a perfect gift for armchair detectives and puzzlers to hone their minds and solve a series of crimes.

title: What to Expect When You're Expecting: (Updated in 2025)
author: Heidi Murkoff
blurb: Your pregnancy explained and your pregnant body demystified, head (what to do about those headaches) to feet (why they’re so swollen), back (how to stop it from aching) to front (why you can’t tell a baby by mom’s bump). Filled with must-have information, practical advice, realistic insight, easy-to-use tips, and lots of reassurance, you’ll also find the very latest on prenatal screenings, which medications are safe, and the most current birthing options—from water birth to gentle c-sections.

title: The Let Them Theory: A Life-Changing Tool That Millions of People Can't Stop Talking About
author: Mel Robbins and Sawyer Robbins
blurb: Two simple words—Let Them—will set you free. Free from the opinions, drama, and judgments of others. Free from the exhausting cycle of trying to manage everything and everyone around you. The Let Them Theory puts the power to create a life you love back in your hands—and this book will show you exactly how to do it.

title: Dogs, Boys, and Other Things I’ve Cried About
author: Isabel Klee
blurb: From the social media superstar behind @SimonSits, Isabel Klee—known for her heartwarming tales of dog rescue—comes an utterly winning memoir about a twentysomething woman’s search for true love in New York City and the dogs who helped her find it.

title: How to Start: Discovering Your Life's Work
author: Jodi Kantor
blurb: With warmth, honesty, and inspired wisdom, Pulitzer Prize winning journalist Jodi Kantor expands on her triumphant Columbia University commencement address, tackling the question, "How, in this environment, is anyone supposed to find and start their life's work?”

title: Rewired: The Breakthrough Tapping Method to Stop Overthinking, Calm Your Body, and Finally Feel at Ease
author: Nick Ortner and Jessica Ortner
blurb: The New York Times best-selling authors behind The Tapping Solution reframe their groundbreaking program with the latest science and case studies to help you shift out of survival mode and reclaim your emotional freedom.

title: Atomic Habits
author: James Clear
blurb: No matter your goals, Atomic Habits offers a proven framework for improving—every day. James Clear, one of the world’s leading experts on habit formation, reveals practical strategies that will teach you exactly how to form good habits, break bad ones, and master the tiny behaviors that lead to remarkable results.

title: Strangers: A Memoir of Marriage
author: Belle Burden
blurb: With unflinching honesty and profound grace, Burden charts a path through heartbreak to show the power of a woman who refuses to give up on love. Strangers is a stunning, deeply moving, compulsively readable memoir heralding the arrival of a thrilling new literary talent.

title: Start With Yourself: A New Vision for Work & Life
author: Emma Grede
blurb: Start With Yourself is a game-changing, no-BS guide for anyone seeking meaningful success on their own terms. It’s an essential framework that will give you the tools and mindset to unlock your full potential in life and business.

title: Famesick: A Memoir
author: Lena Dunham
blurb: In this rowdy, frank reflection on illness, fame, sex, and everything in between, the remarkable mind behind the hit series Girls and the bestselling author of Not That Kind of Girl asks whether fulfilling her creative ambitions has been worth the pain.

title: Man's Search for Meaning
author: Viktor E. Frankl
blurb: A book for finding purpose and strength in times of great despair, the international best-seller is still just as relevant today as when it was first published.

title: London Falling: A Mysterious Death in a Gilded City and a Family's Search for Truth
author: Patrick Radden Keefe
blurb: From the bestselling, prizewinning author of Say Nothing and Empire of Pain, a spellbinding account of a family devastated by the sudden death of their nineteen-year-old son, only to discover that he had created a secret life which drew him into the dangerous criminal underworld that lies beneath London’s glittering surface.

title: The Psychology of Money: Timeless lessons on wealth, greed, and happiness
author: Morgan Housel
blurb: In The Psychology of Money, award-winning author Morgan Housel shares 19 short stories exploring the different ways people think about money and teaches you how to make better sense of one of life's most important topics.

title: The 48 Laws of Power
author: Robert Greene
blurb: Some laws teach the need for prudence ("Law 1: Never Outshine the Master"), others teach the value of confidence ("Law 28: Enter Action with Boldness"), and many recommend absolute self-preservation ("Law 15: Crush Your Enemy Totally"). Every law, though, has one thing in common: an interest in total domination. In a bold and arresting two-color backage, The 48 Laws of Power is ideal whether your aim is conquest, self-defense, or simply to understand the rules of the game.

title: The Only Living Trusts Book You’ll Ever Need: How to Make Your Own Living Trust, Avoid Probate & Protect Your Heirs
author: Garrett Monroe
blurb: Don’t leave the future of your estate up to chance. This is your toolkit to ensure your assets are managed exactly as you wish, without the interference of courts or the burden of unnecessary taxes.

title: Into the Wood Chipper: A Whistleblower's Account of How the Trump Administration Shredded USAID
author: Nicholas Enrich
blurb: Nicholas Enrich had finally achieved his lifelong dream: becoming USAID’s lead official for global health. But that dream turned out to be a nightmare in the tumultuous time after President Trump’s second inauguration.

title: The American Nightmare: How Inflation, Debt, and Banks Hijacked the American Dream
author: John (Coach JV) Vasquez
blurb: What if everything you’ve been taught about money, success, and security is about to change… forever? This is your guide to navigating the new financial world.

title: Die With Zero: Getting All You Can from Your Money and Your Life
author: Bill Perkins
blurb: Using his own life experiences as well as the inspiring stories and cautionary tales of others—and drawing on eye-opening insights about time, money, and happiness from psychological science and behavioral finance—Perkins makes a timely, convincing, and contrarian case for living large.

title: The Meaning of Your Life: Finding Purpose in an Age of Emptiness
author: Arthur C. Brooks
blurb: From the #1 New York Times bestselling author of From Strength to Strength, the definitive account of how the modern world makes meaning so hard to find—and a plan to discover your life’s deepest purpose.

title: Unreasonable Hospitality: The Remarkable Power of Giving People More Than They Expect
author: Will Guidara
blurb: Today, every business can choose to be a hospitality business—and we can all transform ordinary transactions into extraordinary experiences. Featuring sparkling stories of his journey through restaurants, with the industry’s most famous players like Daniel Boulud and Danny Meyer, Guidara urges us all to find the magic in what we do.

title: Meditations
author: Marcus Aurelius, translation by James Harris
blurb: Meditations is a series of personal writings by Marcus Aurelius, Roman Emperor from 161 to 180 AD, recording his private notes to himself and ideas on Stoic philosophy.

title: Never Split the Difference: Negotiating As If Your Life Depended On It―Unlock Your Persuasion Potential in Professional and Personal Life
author: Chris Voss and Tahl Raz
blurb: This international bestseller, with more than 3 million copies sold, offers a field-tested approach to high-stakes negotiations—whether in the boardroom, in your community, or at home.

title: Eyes From Penumbra: How Doing Hard Time Awakened My Soul, Freed My Mind, and Opened My Heart
author: Adam Lacerda
blurb:
The powerful true story of a young man’s journey through a 27-year prison sentence that offers profound insights into society’s deepest struggles

Adam Lacerda thought he understood the world—how to succeed in it, how to navigate it, how to control it. Then, in a single moment, everything collapsed.

Sentenced to decades behind bars, Lacerda is thrust into a brutal and surreal reality—an institutional underworld governed by unspoken rules, shifting alliances, and unchecked power. Inside, survival is not guaranteed, justice is anything but blind, and the line between order and chaos is razor thin. But as the walls close in, something unexpected begins to emerge: clarity.

In Eyes from Penumbra, Lacerda delivers a gripping, unfiltered account of life inside the American prison system—exposing its dysfunction, its hidden hierarchies, and its moral contradictions. Through moments of violence, reflection, and unlikely human connection, Lacerda confronts the deeper forces that shape not only the incarcerated, but all of us: our capacity for self-destruction, our search for meaning, and the fragile distance between who we are and who we might become. What he discovers is both unsettling and profoundly hopeful—that even in the darkest places, the truest version of ourselves is waiting to be revealed.

Raw, provocative, and ultimately transformative, Eyes from Penumbra challenges everything you think you know about justice, identity, and redemption—and asks a question that lingers long after the final page:

What does it really mean to be free?

title: Grafting
author: Rylan Hynes
blurb:
A luminous debut novel about second chances, forbidden love, and the courage to cultivate beauty in unforgiving soil.

When Eben Turner returns to his family's apple orchard in rural Maine after his parents' death, he faces more than just the weight of inheritance―he confronts the love he left behind. A decade has passed since he and Chris Hartley, the Methodist minister's son, were torn apart in their final year of high school, their secret relationship severed by the forces of faith and small-town judgment. Now, as adults navigating the crossroads of identity and belonging, they must reckon with both their buried past and an uncertain future.

Set against the Edenic beauty of New England, Grafting unfolds through dual perspectives in a lyrical narrative that mirrors the delicate art of joining two trees to create something stronger. As Eben and Chris work to understand how they came to be parted, they discover that healing requires more than forgiveness―it demands the courage to cultivate love in hostile ground.

This exquisite debut explores the cost of forbidden desire and the resilience required to claim one's authentic self. With prose as rooted and enduring as the heritage apple trees that anchor the story, Grafting asks whether two people can truly come home to each other after being forced apart by the very communities that shaped them.

title: PULSE
author: Maria Nazos
blurb:
Named for the Pulse nightclub shooting and the rhythm of heartbeats, this collection of poems grapples with finding life amid profound losses.
 
The poems of PULSE look deeply into a troubled world, inspecting what makes us suffer and demonstrating how we overcome difficulties. PULSE interrogates painful losses of friends to cancer, harmful politics, hate crimes, and mass shootings, including the 2016 massacre at Pulse nightclub. Maria Nazos examines the life force that continues to pulse relentlessly through a fragmented world, that enables us to flow, breathe, and regenerate, even through grief and loss. From Provincetown beaches and Costa Rican crab shacks to Midwestern plains and a Tampa nightclub, the collection moves through madness, redemption, and love.

title: Capable: How to Teach Your Kids the Strengths, Skills, and Strategies to Build Resilience
author: Sissy Goff and David Thomas
blurb:
Discover the Missing Piece of Your Child's Ability to Thrive

Today's kids face unprecedented levels of anxiety, depression, and emotional distress. Despite our best efforts, our children continue to struggle with internal and external pressures that impact their mental health. If we're doing all we can to make sure our kids are happy, why aren't they?

Beloved therapists, speakers, and authors Sissy Goff and David Thomas have discovered the key is found in one word: capable. Capable kids are confident kids who don't shrink from challenges. They're resilient kids who get back up when life knocks them down. They're thriving kids because they have a deep belief in their ability to do hard things, overcome roadblocks, and keep moving forward.

In Capable, Sissy and David draw on their more than fifty years of combined counseling experience to offer a clinically sound, emotionally attuned, faith-informed approach to help kids build the resilience they need in today's world. Through evidence-based insight and practical guidance, Sissy and David equip you to nurture capability--the internalized belief that a child can face adversity, navigate challenges, manage stress, and build confidence in who they are and who God has made them to be.

title: A Pen to Change the World: The Life of J.K. Rowling
subgroup: Bruce Wagner's Pick of the Week
author: Solomon Schmidt
blurb: The “wunderkind” Solomon Schmidt—known for his prolific, precociously empathic and thoughtful snapshots and interviews with famous people (Mike Tyson, Jane Goodall)—has also been called “the youngest child historian in America.” Goodall herself said, “I have admiration for that Solomon is doing to make this a better world. A more educated world.” In A Pen to Change the World, he takes on another multilayered, brilliant subject: J. K. Rowling. The average person made know just two things about the astonishing Rowling: that she wrote much of the Harry Potter books in a coffeeshop; and that she is “transphobic”—the former is true, the latter is an absurd falsehood bull-horned to the world by malevolent extremists, malcontents, and even some of the young, impressionable actors she gave careers to. Because of her strength, the one-time single mom and current philanthropist weathered that tempest in the woke teapot and moved on, for which we can be grateful. In Schmidt’s book, we learn much about the iconic woman that we may not have known otherwise. Filled with copious photographs and interviews with friends and blood relations, detailing her formative encounters with abuse, depression, and death in her twenties, A Pen to Change the World will enlighten and entertain those who read its pages. I urge you to become one of them.

title: A Pen to Change the World: The Life of J.K. Rowling
author: Solomon Schmidt
blurb:
Solomon Schmidt was twenty-one years old when he began writing this book. He is the author of nine previous books, including eight in the History Bites series and Legal Gladiator, the authorized biography of Alan Dershowitz. He was described at thirteen as “the youngest child historian in America” by Fox & Friends. Solomon has traveled to all seven continents to film videos for his History Bites YouTube channel, has lectured at nearly fifty conferences throughout the US, and runs historical tours in England and Scotland. His work has been praised by Jocko Willink, Glenn Greenwald, Bill Potter, Steven Pinker, Nima Rinji Sherpa, John Solomon, Jay Sekulow, Robert Shapiro, Mike Huckabee, Jamie Raskin, Mike Pompeo, Noam Chomsky, the late Jane Goodall, and Pres. Donald Trump, among many others. He lives near Buffalo, New York.

“An amazing young author.”
—Mike Tyson

“Solomon is a prodigy. He follows in the tradition of David McCullough - objective, down-the-line, and fair. To this day I still don't know what [his] politics are. I think he will be the greatest biographer of the 21st century.”
—Professor Alan Dershowitz

“[Solomon has] quite a remarkable record. [I'm] really impressed.”
—Dr. Noam Chomsky

An Excerpt from A Pen to Change the World

The café had a red exterior with a golden elephant on one of the front windows, and Rowling entered with her stroller to find an interior with red walls bearing artwork highlighted by lamps, the clatter of activity audible around her. When she chose to take a seat at one of the tables in the back, behind her was a high window through which she could see the turrets and rampart of Edinburgh Castle.

“In my opinion, the best writing café is just crowded enough so that you blend in, but not so crowded that you end up sharing a table with somebody who tries to read [your work] upside down, has staff who don’t glower at you if you sit there too long . . . and doesn’t play very loud music,” described Rowling.

Her sister’s husband, Roger, had recently opened a café called Nicolson’s, and Rowling figured that she would not be as much of a nuisance there, what with her purchasing a single cup of coffee. There were “so many tables” in Nicolson’s that “I didn’t feel too guilty about taking [one] up too long,” she said. She avoided visiting her brother-in-law’s café during what she knew were their busy periods. Walking through the blue-and-yellow dining area, the walls bearing papier-mâché flowers, she took a seat at her favorite table in the corner.

Nicolson’s was housed on the second floor of a building across from the imposing, columned Old College of the University of Edinburgh. Rowling loved looking down at the street below, which was filled with traffic and pedestrians and which ended at the famous Balmoral Hotel beyond.

Knowing that additional cups might put her over the edge, she sipped a cup or two of cold espresso at her corner table and wrote in long-hand. She liked using a black ballpoint pen best if she had one. It was warm in the café, and rain sometimes pattered on the windows. Inscribing her ideas onto a piece of paper, she concentrated her mind on completing Book One while Jessica remained napping. “You can get a hell of a lot of writing done in two hours if you know that’s the only chance you are going to get,” said Rowling.

One sunny afternoon, she ducked into Nicolson’s after Jessica dozed off, took her seat, and flew through an entire chapter of the book. She wrote what ultimately became Chapter Ten, “Halloween,” in one sitting, and unusually changed almost nothing about it after review. In the chapter, Rowling had Harry and Ron face down a troll who had trapped Hermione in the girl’s bathroom. When the boys are reprimanded by Professor McGonagall after fighting the troll by themselves, Hermione takes the blame for the incident, thereby winning the respect of Harry and Ron, who had become fed up with her bossy, know-it-all attitude. “Hermione, bless her, is so very annoying in the early part of ‘Philosopher’s Stone’ that I really felt it needed something (literally) huge to bring her together with Harry and Ron,” commented Rowling.

According to Nicolson’s general manager Roland Thomson, the staff at Nicolson’s all knew “Joanne and Jessica.”

“They would come in almost every day, and the wee girl would sleep while her mother wrote,” he said. Rowling appreciated the friendly and patient staff members at Nicolson’s, and she joked about helping to make the café famous if her book got picked up. “Well, you know, [if ] it gets published, I’ll try and get you loads of publicity,” she would humorously declare.

Apart from when she was experiencing one of her especially grim moments, Rowling passionately believed in her book, at times heading home down Leith Walk feeling elated at the writing she had churned out that day. “People would ask me what I had been doing and I would say just out walking,” said Rowling. “I think they thought I was very strange and possibly depressed.”

One time, after a caffeine-fueled writing session, she heard a seemingly prophetic voice speak inside her head while on the North-South Bridges near Nicolson’s. “The difficulty will be in getting this published,” the voice told her. “But if it’s published, there will be a handful of people who really love it.”

“Oh come on!” Rowling internally replied to the voice in disbelief. She snapped back into outer reality on the Bridges, nearby the Balmoral Hotel. The hotel was a luxurious, Victorian-era establishment with a silver stone clocktower—where the rich could rest their heads for one night at a sum that was greater than the amount Rowling and Jessica lived on for one week through welfare.

While scraping by and feeling anger over her many failures, Rowling said that she felt like a “waste of space” of a human being.

Joanne Rowling certainly took up space (however small). She took up a unit in a block of apartment buildings and utilized a bed to sleep next to her child when night came on. She covered her body with clothes obtained from a thrift shop. In order to continue existing, she consumed precious supplies of air and food. She used water to wash herself and required diapers to cover her impoverished baby’s bottom. Rowling placed her feet inside a pair of black boots with laces when she decided to go out and step on the city pavement on the way to a café. And at these cafés, she topped it all by using up a coffee, table, and piece of paper while sitting and engaging in her pastime of writing a story about a boy who goes to magic school. (She also took up space in public bathrooms, once jotting a paragraph of Harry Potter while crouching on the public toilet.)

Twenty-nine-year-old, red-haired, blue-eyed Joanne Rowling was one of billions of insignificants. She could make no fashion statement—people would have given no glance at her clothing and attire. She would have known that any makeup or jewelry or accessories she might don, whether the eyeliner or dangly earrings she enjoyed wearing, would not turn her into a person important enough to make a fashion statement (and, of course, she could not don those articles even if she wanted to, what with her dependence on secondhand clothes). She would have understood that most of the people walking by her on the street did not care about her opinions on politics or the economy or the state of the world they shared. When she died, she would join the ranks of dispensable people interred in cemeteries.

But Rowling had her two babies with her: Jessica, “whom I adored,” she said, and her beloved book—“my baby,” as she described it once. Suspecting that she knew how to tell a story, if nothing else, she committed herself to doing right by Harry Potter at the least.
